title: Trout meunière
subtitle: Trout fillets dredged in flour, fried in butter, finished with brown butter and lemon.
excerpt: A short bistro classic. Trout fillets are dusted with seasoned flour, fried in foaming butter until the skin crisps, then served with the brown butter from the pan, a squeeze of lemon, and chopped parsley. Eight minutes of attention.
sourceNotes: Meunière (literally, 'in the manner of the miller's wife') is the standard French preparation for flat or fillet fish: dredged in flour, fried in butter, finished with lemon and parsley. The earliest French codifications appear in Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903), where it is the template against which other fish preparations are described. Mrs Beeton (Book of Household Management, 1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) gives the same method for fried trout: dust with flour, fry in butter, serve with lemon. In British kitchens the dish entered domestic cooking through Elizabeth David's writing in the 1950s.

content.text: Two trout fillets, a small handful of flour, eighty grams of butter, half a lemon, a little parsley. The dish is older than the named technique and works on whatever flat or fillet fish you have to hand: trout, plaice, lemon sole, sea bream. The skin should be on; the bones should be out.
The whole thing turns on the butter. Half goes into the pan to fry the fish; the rest goes in at the end and cooks past golden to light brown,

. A pan that is too cool gives you fish poached in dairy. A pan that is too hot burns the butter before the fillet cooks through. Medium-high heat, butter foaming but not smoking, is the temperature you want.

items.name: Trout fillet
items.prepNote: skin on, pin-boned, around 150 g each

content.text: A 26 cm frying pan and a fish slice. The flour goes on a flat plate, not in a bag; you want to lay the fillets down and turn them over rather than shake them.

Pat the trout fillets dry on both sides with a piece of kitchen paper. Wet skin will not crisp. Tip the {{plain-flour}} of flour onto a flat plate and season with a pinch of salt and a turn of pepper. Lay each fillet in the flour skin-side down first, then turn over so both sides take a thin even coat. Tap off the excess; only a film of flour should remain. The fillets are ready to cook now and should not sit in the flour any longer than that, or the coating goes pasty.

Put 40 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} into the pan over medium-high heat. The butter will melt, foam, and the foam will subside as the water cooks out. When the foam has just started to drop and the butter smells nutty, lay the fillets in skin-side down. Press lightly with the back of the fish slice for the first ten seconds so the skin makes flat contact with the pan. Cook for three to four minutes, until the skin is crisp and the flesh has turned opaque about two-thirds of the way up the fillet. Turn carefully and cook the flesh side for one minute more. Lift onto warm plates.

Wipe the pan if any flour has burnt and is stuck to it; otherwise leave the residue. Drop the remaining 40 g of {{unsalted-butter}} in over medium heat. Swirl the pan. The butter will foam a second time, then the foam will subside and the colour will deepen from yellow to gold to light brown. As soon as it smells of toasted nuts, lift off the heat. Add the {{capers}} of capers, the {{parsley}} of parsley, and a squeeze of half the {{lemon}}. Stand back: the lemon juice spits in the hot butter. Pour the brown butter sauce over the fillets and serve with the second lemon half on the plate.

items.fix: Let the skin set on the pan for a full three minutes before trying to turn. If the skin resists the fish slice, it is not ready. Once crisp it releases cleanly.
items.cause: Pan or butter not hot enough when the fillet went in; or the fillet was moved too soon.
items.symptom: Skin sticks to the pan when turning

items.fix: Fillets under 1 cm thick need only thirty seconds on the flesh side, sometimes none. Pull as soon as the centre is opaque.
items.cause: Fillet was thin and was left in the pan too long after turning.

items.fix: Discard the burnt butter and start the brown-butter stage again with fresh. There is no rescue for burnt butter; the taste carries through.
items.cause: Pan too hot, or the butter was left unattended past the point of brown.

items.fix: Dredge and cook in the same motion. Tap off the excess before laying in the pan.
items.cause: Too much flour, or the fillets sat in the flour before cooking.
items.symptom: Coating is pasty rather than crisp

items.fix: Add the lemon while the pan is still warm and swirl to bring the sauce back together.
items.cause: Lemon added away from the heat; the cold acid shocked the emulsion.
items.symptom: Brown butter splits when the lemon goes in

content.text: The same method works for plaice, lemon sole, sea bream, and small whole mackerel. Adjust the cooking time to the thickness of the fillet. For amandine, scatter 30 g of flaked almonds into the pan with the second butter and let them toast in the brown butter before the lemon goes in.

Meunière sits at the start of the French canon of fish cookery. It is the first preparation taught and the one against which every other is measured. The dish is plain enough that it depends entirely on freshness of fish, control of the pan, and the precise moment of stopping the butter. Served traditionally with steamed new potatoes and a green vegetable; no sauce other than the brown butter is needed.
